 {"id":"wa-2dd4s.4","title":"Improved constraint-based resize handling","description":"# Improved Constraint-Based Resize Handling

## Why
WezTerm uses proportional resizing: when a split is resized, children resize proportionally. This doesn't respect minimum sizes and can make panes unusably small. Zellij uses constraint-based resizing which is better.

## What to build

### Constraint system
Each pane has constraints:
```rust
pub struct PaneConstraints {
    pub min_width: u16,   // Default: 5 columns
    pub min_height: u16,  // Default: 3 rows
    pub max_width: Option<u16>,
    pub max_height: Option<u16>,
    pub preferred_width: Option<u16>,
    pub preferred_height: Option<u16>,
    pub fixed: bool,      // Don't resize this pane
}
```

### Constraint solver
When terminal is resized:
1. Compute total available space
2. Assign minimum sizes to all panes
3. Distribute remaining space proportionally (respecting max constraints)
4. If not enough space for minimums: collapse least-priority panes
5. Use topological sort on the split tree for constraint propagation

### Priority-based collapse
When terminal is too small for all panes:
```rust
pub enum CollapsePriority {
    Never,      // This pane never collapses (e.g., main editor)
    Low,        // Collapse first (e.g., status bar)
    Normal,     // Default
    High,       // Collapse last
}
```
Collapsed panes become stacked (hidden but accessible via tab).

### Resize edge detection
When user drags a split border:
- Determine which panes are affected
- Apply constraint solver to just that subtree
- Snap to character boundaries
- Preview resize before applying (render ghost borders)

## Files affected
- frankenterm/mux/src/tab.rs (resize logic — currently proportional)
- frankenterm/mux/src/pane.rs (add constraints to pane)
- frankenterm/bintree/ (tree operations may need constraint awareness)

## Testing
- Resize terminal to various sizes, verify no pane below minimum
- Test constraint propagation through split tree
- Test priority-based collapse (correct pane collapses first)
- Test fixed panes don't resize
- Benchmark: resize calculation should be <1ms
- Property test: sum of pane widths always equals terminal width

## Test framework requirements

### proptest — resize constraint satisfaction
Use proptest to generate arbitrary split trees with random constraints and terminal sizes, then verify:
- Minimum sizes are always respected: no pane is smaller than its min_width/min_height
- Total area is preserved: sum of all pane widths in a horizontal split equals parent width (and analogously for vertical splits)
- Fixed panes never change size after any resize operation
- Constraint solver is deterministic: same inputs always produce same layout
- Collapse priority is respected: higher-priority panes collapse only after all lower-priority panes have collapsed

### criterion benchmarks — resize computation
Benchmark with criterion:
- Constraint solver execution time for N-pane split tree (target: <500us for 50 panes)
- Resize edge detection for a split tree with M borders (target: <100us)
- Full resize cycle: terminal resize event to layout committed (target: <1ms)

## Cross-references
- **wa-1xk9j** — Zellij layout engine analysis: architecture study that informs the constraint-based resize design, including Zellij's constraint propagation algorithm

 {"id":"wa-2dd4s.5","title":"FrankenMux integration — hybrid muxer assembly and wire protocol","description":"# FrankenMux Integration — Hybrid Muxer Assembly

## Why
This is the capstone bead that brings all FrankenMux innovations together into a cohesive hybrid muxer. After individual features (floating panes, swap layouts, constraint resize) are implemented, this bead integrates them with the existing WezTerm mux protocol.

## What to build

### Unified mux model
The FrankenMux tab model combines all features:
```rust
pub struct FrankenTab {
    // WezTerm foundation
    pane_tree: Tree,              // Split-based pane tree (WezTerm)
    
    // Zellij innovations
    floating_panes: Vec<FloatingPane>,  // Overlay panes
    pane_stacks: HashMap<NodeId, Vec<Arc<dyn Pane>>>,  // Stacked panes per position
    layouts: Vec<Layout>,         // Swap layout definitions
    current_layout: usize,        // Active layout index
    
    // FrankenTerm originals  
    constraints: HashMap<PaneId, PaneConstraints>,  // Per-pane resize constraints
    pane_budgets: HashMap<PaneId, MemoryBudget>,     // Per-pane memory (from wa-3axa)
    event_hooks: Vec<HookRegistration>,  // WASM/Lua hooks (from wa-3dfxb)
}
```

### Wire protocol extensions
Extend the codec PDU set for new features:
- All floating pane PDUs
- Layout swap PDU
- Constraint update PDU
- Stack manipulation PDUs
- Ensure backward compat: old clients ignore unknown PDUs

### Migration path
Existing WezTerm mux protocol remains as-is. New features are additive:
- Old clients connecting to FrankenMux server: work fine, don't see floating panes
- New clients connecting to old server: new features silently degrade

## Files affected
- frankenterm/mux/src/tab.rs (FrankenTab integration)
- frankenterm/mux/src/domain.rs (domain-level mux management)
- frankenterm/codec/src/lib.rs (new PDU types)
- frankenterm/mux/src/lib.rs (re-exports)

## Testing
- End-to-end: create tab with splits + floating panes + stacks
- Swap layout preserves all pane types
- Resize respects constraints across all pane types
- Wire protocol round-trip for all new PDUs
- Backward compat: old-format PDUs still work
- Stress test: 50 panes (mixed types) in one tab
- Memory: verify per-pane budgets are enforced

## Alien artifact quality targets
- Formal proof: constraint solver always terminates and produces valid layout
- Property test: layout invariants hold after any sequence of operations
- Zero-allocation steady-state: no allocations during normal pane switching
- Cache-line aware: hot pane data fits in L1 cache line

## Test framework requirements

### LabRuntime DPOR — concurrent mux operations
Use LabRuntime with DPOR exploration to verify:
- Concurrent pane create/destroy across splits, floating, and stacks is linearizable
- Concurrent layout swap + pane creation never loses a pane
- Domain reconnection during active mux operations recovers correctly
- Wire protocol message ordering is preserved under concurrent sends from multiple tabs

### criterion benchmarks — wire protocol throughput
Benchmark with criterion:
- PDU serialize/deserialize round-trip throughput (target: >100K PDUs/sec)
- Wire protocol encode+transmit+decode latency for common PDUs (target: <50us per PDU)
- FrankenTab creation time with N pre-existing panes (target: <1ms for 20 panes)
- Layout swap throughput under concurrent operations

### proptest — protocol message roundtrips
Use proptest to generate arbitrary PDU values for all new message types and verify:
- Serialize then deserialize produces the original PDU (roundtrip identity)
- Unknown/future PDU type codes are safely skipped (forward compatibility)
- Truncated messages produce clean errors, never panics
- All enum variants are covered by the generator

## Cross-references
- **wa-283h4.3** — TLA+ protocol verification: formal verification of the mux wire protocol state machine, ensuring the extended PDU set maintains safety properties (no message loss, no duplicate delivery, correct sequencing)
- **wa-2dd4s.2** — Floating pane overlay system: provides the FloatingPane model integrated here
- **wa-2dd4s.3** — Swap layouts: provides the Layout and pane redistribution logic integrated here
- **wa-2dd4s.4** — Constraint-based resize: provides the PaneConstraints solver integrated here

## Skills: /idea-wizard, /extreme-software-optimization

## What caut does
Tracks LLM provider usage across 16+ services (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, etc.). Monitors rate limits, quotas, credits, plan types. Dual-mode: human-readable or JSON for agents.

## Current state
Standalone CLI at /Users/jemanuel/projects/coding_agent_usage_tracker.

## Integration plan

### 1. Import caut-core as library
- Use caut's provider APIs directly
- Poll usage data on a schedule (every 5 min)
- Cache results in FrankenTerm's state

### 2. Quota-aware pane scheduling
When launching a new agent pane:
- Check available quota for the agent's LLM provider
- If quota < 10% remaining: warn before launch
- If quota exhausted: suggest alternate provider or queue the task

### 3. Automatic credential rotation
When an active agent hits a rate limit:
- Detect rate limit from pane output patterns
- Query caut for available alternative accounts
- Inject new credentials or suggest switching

### 4. Cost dashboard
- Real-time cost tracking across all active agent panes
- Per-agent, per-provider breakdown
- Budget alerts: warn when approaching spending limits
- Expose via MCP tool and TUI widget

### 5. Performance
- Quota check: <10ms (cached)
- Provider API polling: background, non-blocking
- No impact on pane operations

## Tests
- Test quota check on pane launch
- Test rate limit detection from pane output
- Test credential rotation flow
- Test cost aggregation across 200 panes

## Acceptance criteria
- caut-core as library dependency
- Quota check on every pane launch
- Rate limit detection and auto-recovery
- Cost dashboard with per-agent breakdown
- <10ms quota check latency

## Test Framework Requirements
- **Criterion benchmarks**: Tracking overhead benchmarks:
  - Quota check latency: benchmark the cached quota lookup path. Target <10ms p99. Include cold-cache (first check) and warm-cache (subsequent checks) variants.
  - Provider polling overhead: benchmark the background polling cycle for 1, 5, 16 providers. Verify total polling cycle completes in <5s.
  - Cost aggregation: benchmark aggregating cost data across 200 panes. Target <1ms for the aggregation step.
  - Dashboard render data: benchmark preparing the cost dashboard data structure for 200 panes x 16 providers.
- **Proptest for quota calculation correctness**: Property-based tests for quota and cost logic:
  - Generate random usage histories (sequences of API calls with token counts and timestamps), compute remaining quota, and verify it matches the provider's quota formula
  - Generate random multi-provider usage across N agents, compute total cost, and verify it equals the sum of per-agent costs (additivity)
  - Generate random rate limit scenarios (burst usage patterns), verify the quota-aware scheduler correctly blocks launches when quota is exhausted and allows them when quota is available
  - For credential rotation: generate random provider states (some exhausted, some available), verify rotation always selects an available provider or returns "all exhausted"

## Cross-References
- **wa-8eoug** (CPU pressure scheduling): caut's usage data directly informs CPU pressure scheduling decisions. When an agent is rate-limited (caut reports quota exhausted), the CPU scheduler should deprioritize that pane's background tasks since the agent cannot make progress. The two systems should share a "pane health" signal.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-10T16:12:36.822466Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:47:53.630901Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

## Goal
Make the watcher’s health state visible so we can quickly diagnose:
- stuck tailers
- slow polling
- DB backpressure
- pattern match overload

## Metrics (example)
- discovery tick duration
- tailer count + per-domain breakdown
- per-pane last_seen + last_seq
- storage write queue depth
- event bus queue depths
- FTS insert latency

## Deliverables
- `wa status --health` (or `wa health`) prints the snapshot.
- Robot/MCP can access the same snapshot if useful.

## Testing
- Unit/integration tests:
  - health snapshot JSON is schema-parseable and stable
  - “stuck pane” synthetic scenario is surfaced clearly

- CLI contract tests:
  - covered by `wa-nu4.3.2.11` (stable JSON output in non-TTY mode)

- Diagnostic coverage:
  - `wa doctor` tests (`wa-nu4.3.4.8`) should validate that health signals are consistent with watcher state when available.

## Acceptance Criteria
- When a pane stops producing output, health snapshot highlights it clearly.

 {"id":"wa-2ep","title":"[EPIC] Deep Explainability: wa why + Enriched Errors + Decision Tracing","description":"# [EPIC] Deep Explainability System

## Mission
Make every wa decision **transparent and understandable**. When wa allows, denies, or takes any action, users should be able to understand exactly why.

## Why This Is Critical
Terminal automation is scary because it is opaque. The #1 question users ask about any automation is: **"Why did it do that?"**

Without explainability:
- Users distrust the tool
- Debugging requires diving into logs
- Configuration is trial-and-error
- Support burden is high

With explainability:
- Users understand and trust decisions
- Debugging is self-service
- Configuration is informed
- The tool teaches as it operates

## Components

### 1. `wa why` Command
A dedicated command to explain any recent decision:
```bash
$ wa why denied --pane 3
Decision: DENY
Reason: Pane 3 is in AltScreen mode
Evidence:
  - AltScreen flag detected at 2026-01-18T14:32:01
  - Policy rule: safety.alt_screen_block (severity: hard_deny)
  - Rationale: Sending input while AltScreen is active can corrupt TUI applications

To proceed:
  1. Wait for pane to exit AltScreen mode, OR
  2. Use the approval flow:
     - run: wa approve <allow_once_code>
     - retry the original command
```

### 2. Enriched Error Messages
Every error includes actionable remediation, preferring **safe** commands over “delete files” guidance.

Example:
```bash
$ wa watch
Error: Cannot start watcher - workspace lock is held

To fix:
  - See who is running it: wa doctor
  - Stop the watcher safely: wa stop
  - If the lock appears stale, follow wa doctor’s instructions (do not guess)

Then retry: wa watch
```

```bash
$ wa send --pane 99 "hello"
Error: Pane 99 not found

Available panes: 1, 3, 7, 9
Did you mean:   wa send --pane 9 "hello"  (closest match)
List all:       wa status
```

### 3. Decision Context Capture
PolicyEngine and workflow runner capture full decision context (not just result):
- Input conditions (capabilities, state)
- Rules evaluated
- Why each rule matched or did not match
- Final decision with confidence

### 4. Explanation Templates
Human-readable explanations for every rule and decision type:
- Map `(rule_id, decision)` → explanation + remediation
- Keep templates in sync with rules (enforce via tests)

## Design Principles
1. **No jargon**: Explanations use plain English
2. **Actionable**: Every explanation includes "To fix:" steps
3. **Contextual**: Show evidence from the actual situation
4. **Progressive**: Brief summary first, details on request

## Testing
- Unit tests: Every rule has an explanation template
- Integration tests: `wa why` returns expected explanations for known scenarios
- UX tests: Explanations are understandable by non-experts (user testing)

## Success Criteria
- `wa why <decision-id>` (or equivalent) explains any recent policy decision/workflow action
- All error messages include "To fix:" with specific steps
- Explanations point to safe next actions (e.g., `wa stop`, `wa doctor`, `wa approve`) and avoid unsafe “just delete X” advice
- Users can self-diagnose the majority of issues without external help

## Acceptance Criteria
- wa why returns a structured decision trace with redacted context.
- Enriched errors include cause chain and remediation hints.
- Trace output is stable and machine-parseable.

 {"id":"wa-2f2m","title":"Semantic Output Compression","description":"## Goal
Implement semantic output compression that deduplicates and compresses terminal output based on content understanding, not just byte-level compression, dramatically reducing storage and memory for repetitive agent output.

## Background & Motivation
AI agent terminal output is highly repetitive:
- Compilation: same warnings repeated across builds
- Test output: similar pass/fail patterns with minor variations
- Git operations: repeated status checks, diff headers
- Claude Code: repeated tool_use boundaries, system prompts
- Progress updates: "Processing file 1/100", "Processing file 2/100", ...

Byte-level compression (zstd) helps but misses semantic redundancy. For example, 100 "Processing file N/100" lines are semantically ONE template with a counter.

## Technical Design

### Template Detection
```rust
pub struct OutputTemplate {
    /// Template pattern with placeholders
    pub pattern: String,        // e.g., "Processing file {}/100"
    /// Variable positions and their observed values
    pub variables: Vec<TemplateVariable>,
    /// Number of instances matching this template
    pub instance_count: u64,
    /// First and last seen timestamps
    pub first_seen: Instant,
    pub last_seen: Instant,
}

pub struct TemplateVariable {
    pub position: usize,
    pub values: Vec<String>,    // observed values in order
    pub is_monotonic: bool,     // e.g., counter 1, 2, 3, ...
}
```

### Compression Pipeline
```
Raw output → Line grouping → Template extraction → Delta encoding → Storage
```

1. **Line grouping**: Consecutive similar lines grouped together
2. **Template extraction**: LCS (longest common substring) to find template
3. **Delta encoding**: Only store template + variable values
4. **Storage**: Templates stored once, instances reference template ID

### Similarity Detection
```rust
fn lines_similar(a: &str, b: &str) -> bool {
    // Normalized edit distance < threshold
    let distance = edit_distance(a, b);
    let max_len = a.len().max(b.len());
    if max_len == 0 { return true; }
    (distance as f64 / max_len as f64) < 0.3  // 70% similar
}
```

### Storage Format
```rust
pub struct CompressedOutput {
    /// Unique templates
    pub templates: Vec<OutputTemplate>,
    /// Sequence of (template_id, variable_values)
    pub instances: Vec<(usize, Vec<String>)>,
    /// Lines that didn't match any template (stored verbatim)
    pub unique_lines: Vec<(usize, String)>,  // (position, content)
}
```

### Compression Ratios (Expected)
- Progress output (counters): 100:1
- Repeated compilation warnings: 20:1
- Repeated git status: 15:1
- Mixed agent output: 3-5:1
- Unique output: 1:1 (no worse than raw)

### Integration
- Feeds into differential snapshots (wa-3kxe.3): compressed output is smaller to diff
- Feeds into scrollback (smart scrollback bead): compressed templates take less memory
- Pattern detection (patterns.rs): uses same line classification

### Implementation Location
- New: crates/wa-core/src/output_compression.rs
- Integration: snapshot pipeline compresses output before storage
- Integration: scrollback can use templates for memory reduction

## Existing Code References
- wa-3kxe.3 (differential snapshots): consumes compressed output
- patterns.rs: line classification for template extraction hints
- storage.rs: SQLite storage for templates and instances

## Configuration
```toml
[output_compression]
enabled = true
similarity_threshold = 0.3   # Edit distance ratio for "similar" lines
min_group_size = 3            # Minimum repetitions to create template
max_templates = 1000          # Maximum templates per pane
```

## Dependencies
- Enhances differential snapshots (wa-3kxe.3): smaller diffs
- Enhances scrollback memory: templates compress repetitive content
- patterns.rs: reuse line classification

## Acceptance Criteria
- Template extraction from repetitive output
- Delta encoding: template + variable values
- Compression ratios: >10:1 for progress output, >3:1 for mixed
- Decompression: lossless reconstruction of original output
- Integration with snapshot pipeline
- Unit tests: synthetic repetitive output, verify compression ratio and lossless roundtrip
- Benchmark: compression time < 1ms per 100 lines

## Benchmark Requirements
- **criterion benchmarks** with target `benches/semantic_compression.rs`:
  - `compression_ratio_progress`: measure compression ratio for 1,000 progress-counter lines ("Processing file N/1000"). Target: >50:1 ratio.
  - `compression_ratio_mixed`: measure compression ratio for mixed agent output (errors, warnings, status, unique lines). Target: >3:1 ratio.
  - `compression_throughput`: measure lines processed per second for the full pipeline (grouping + template extraction + delta encoding). Target: >100K lines/sec.
  - `decompression_throughput`: measure reconstruction throughput from compressed format. Target: >200K lines/sec (decompression should be faster than compression).
  - `template_extraction_latency`: measure time to extract a template from a group of 100 similar lines via LCS. Target: <500us per group.

## Property-Based Testing (proptest)
- **Lossless roundtrip invariant**: for any arbitrary string input, `decompress(compress(input)) == input` byte-for-byte. No information is ever lost through the compression pipeline.
- **Compression non-expansion**: compressed output size is always <= original output size + a small fixed overhead (template metadata). Compression never makes output significantly larger.
- **Template stability**: adding one more instance of an existing pattern to a group does not change the template (only adds to the variable values list). Templates are stable under incremental additions.
- **Similarity symmetry**: `lines_similar(a, b) == lines_similar(b, a)` for all string pairs.

## Cross-References
- **wa-283h4.15** (Shannon entropy accounting): provides information-theoretic bounds on achievable compression ratios; semantic compression should approach these bounds for highly repetitive content. Entropy measurements from wa-283h4.15 can guide when semantic compression is worthwhile vs when raw zstd suffices.
- **wa-n9cp** (Content-addressable output dedup): complementary layer — wa-n9cp deduplicates identical segments at the storage level, while wa-2f2m compresses similar-but-not-identical content within segments. Together they handle both exact and fuzzy redundancy.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-09T22:45:16.745779Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-03-28T06:32:04.560373Z","closed_at":"2026-03-28T06:32:04.560143Z","close_reason":"Implemented: output_compression.rs (1095 lines) with semantic output compression + byte_compression.rs","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-2f2m","depends_on_id":"wa-3cyp","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-09T22:45:34.124398Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"wa-2f2m","depends_on_id":"wa-3kxe.3","type":"related","created_at":"2026-02-09T22:45:54.215058Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.8.3","title":"Sync push/pull for config + binary (explicit, non-destructive)","description":"# Task: Sync push/pull for config + binary (feature sync)

## Goal
Implement the core sync operations for:
- `~/.config/wa/**` (config)
- optional `wa` binary (explicit opt-in)

The intent is to make it easy to keep multiple machines consistent **without ever surprising the user**.

## Design principles
- **Explicitness over magic**: no background sync; only `push`/`pull`.
- **Plan-first**: always compute and display a plan; require `--apply` for real changes.
- **No silent overwrites**: overwriting requires confirmation and clear diff metadata.
- **Secret-aware**: never sync forbidden paths; never print secret contents.

## Path selection rules
- Config root is the canonical wa config directory.
- Include only known-safe config artifacts.
- Exclude patterns like:
  - `**/.env*`
  - `**/*token*`
  - `**/*secret*`
  - keychains/SSH keys

(Exact allow/deny rules are authored in the sync spec bead; this bead implements them.)

## Conflict semantics
When both sides changed:
- classify as `Conflict` and refuse to apply automatically
- provide remediation:
  - show which file conflicted
  - suggest manual resolution steps
  - (future) offer an interactive merge/choose-side mode

## Binary sync
- Treat binary sync as a separate category:
  - opt-in in config
  - prefer syncing from a trusted build machine to others
- Validate binary integrity (hash/size) before replacing.

## Testing
- Covered by `wa-nu4.3.8.5` (sync tests):
  - offline push/pull planning and dry-run output stability
  - conflict classification (no silent overwrite)
  - secret/path deny rules

## Acceptance Criteria
- `wa sync push/pull` computes a deterministic plan for config + binary categories.
- `--dry-run` never modifies local or remote files.
- Applying changes requires `--apply` and refuses to overwrite without explicit confirmation.
- Conflicts are detected and surfaced as `Conflict` items (no auto-overwrite).

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.8.4","title":"Sync exported DB snapshots (immutable artifacts, versioned filenames)","description":"# Task: Sync exported DB snapshots (immutable artifacts)

## Goal
Make it easy to move **database snapshots** between machines for debugging or continuity, without ever touching the live DB file.

## Safety model
- Only sync snapshots created via `wa export` / `wa diag bundle` (explicit export paths).
- Never sync the live DB file or WAL/SHM.
- Treat snapshots as immutable artifacts:
  - versioned filenames
  - no in-place overwrite
  - optional compression (if/when supported by export)

## Naming & versioning
- Filenames should encode enough metadata to be self-describing:
  - wa version
  - created timestamp (UTC)
  - workspace key (hashed/short)
  - optional host

## Push/pull behavior
- `push` uploads snapshot(s) to the target under an artifacts directory.
- `pull` downloads snapshots and places them under a local artifacts directory.
- Default behavior is additive (no overwrites).

## UX expectations
- Plans should clearly distinguish:
  - “syncing snapshots” (safe)
  - “syncing live DB” (forbidden; should never appear)

## Testing
- Covered by `wa-nu4.3.8.5` (sync tests):
  - live DB paths are rejected
  - snapshot filenames are stable and never overwritten
  - push/pull round-trips via local-directory backend

## Acceptance Criteria
- `wa sync push` can push an exported snapshot to a target without overwriting existing files.
- `wa sync pull` can pull the same snapshot back and preserves the filename.
- Attempts to sync live DB paths are rejected by the planner.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.8.5","title":"Sync tests: config diffing + dry-run output stability (feature sync)","description":"# Task: Sync tests (feature sync)

## Goal
Make `wa sync` safe and boring by locking down:
- **path selection rules** (what may sync vs must never sync)
- **plan generation** (what would happen)
- **dry-run output stability** (humans + automation can trust it)

Sync is inherently risky: it touches configs and binaries, and can easily become a secret-exfiltration or accidental-overwrite footgun. Tests are the safety net.

## Scope
- Unit + integration tests that run **offline** and **deterministically**.
- No real SSH, no real remote machines, no real secrets.

## Test strategy
### 1) Pure unit tests (no filesystem required)
- **Path allow/deny rules**
  - allow: `~/.config/wa/**` (except explicit denylist)
  - allow (optional): `wa` binary (explicit opt-in)
  - deny: any known secret file patterns (tokens, keyrings, `.env`, etc.)
- **Target resolution**
  - parse configured sync targets
  - explicit “default target” selection rules
  - stable ordering (targets and files are sorted deterministically)
- **Planning semantics**
  - classify changes as `Add` / `Update` / `Skip` / `Conflict`
  - ensure “would overwrite” requires an explicit confirmation gate

### 2) Filesystem integration tests (temp dirs)
- Build a temp “local” tree and a temp “remote” tree (local-directory transport backend).
- Exercise:
  - `push --dry-run` produces a plan but changes nothing
  - `pull --dry-run` produces a plan but changes nothing
  - “same content” yields a minimal/no-op plan
  - “different content” yields Update items (but still requires confirmation in non-dry-run)

### 3) Golden tests for dry-run output stability
- Snapshot the **dry-run plan output** (both modes):
  - TTY-ish human format (no ANSI in tests; use the plain renderer)
  - machine JSON format (stable schema + stable ordering)
- The golden tests should assert:
  - stable ordering of files/targets
  - stable wording for safety warnings
  - redaction is applied to any values that might contain secrets

## Logging & debuggability requirements
- Tests should capture and assert a few critical log lines:
  - which target selected
  - how many items in plan
  - whether an overwrite would have occurred
- Log output must never include file contents or secret values.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `cargo test --features sync` includes deterministic tests that cover:
  - path selection allow/deny rules (including a “secret-ish” fixture)
  - plan generation for push + pull (no-op, update, conflict)
  - dry-run output stability (goldens/snapshots)
- Tests are fully offline (no SSH/network), fast, and reproducible.
- A regression that would accidentally sync a forbidden path fails loudly.


## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Add an explicit assertion that no files are modified in dry-run tests (e.g., hash trees before/after).
  - Include at least one fixture that resembles a secret and ensure it is denied and never appears in output.

-{"id":"wa-nu4.3.9","title":"[EPIC] Ship readiness: docs, CI checks, release workflow, versioning","description":"# [EPIC] Ship readiness (docs + CI + release)

## Mission
Make v0.1.0 shippable:
- clear docs
- reliable CI signals
- predictable version/build metadata

## Why
A tool that controls terminals needs high trust and clear operator UX.

## Definition of done
- `cargo fmt --check`, `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`, `cargo test` pass in CI.
- README + quickstart lets another engineer install and run wa.
- Version/build metadata is visible (`wa --version`).

## Success Criteria
- CI runs fmt/clippy/tests/e2e reliably and preserves artifacts on failure.
- Release workflow/versioning produces reproducible binaries with build metadata.
- Docs/README/quickstart are sufficient for a new contributor to install and run wa.

## Testing
- CI verification:
  - CI must run the full quality gate (fmt/clippy/tests) and E2E, and upload artifacts on failure.

- Release smoke tests:
  - After packaging, run `wa --version` and `wa --help` and a minimal `wa doctor --format json` scenario to ensure the binary is functional.

## Acceptance Criteria
- All items in the following sections are satisfied: Success Criteria, Definition of Done.
- All Testing requirements pass (unit/integration/E2E) with the logging/artifacts described above.
- Any explicit dependencies for this bead are implemented and validated via tests or E2E artifacts.

## Cross-references
- **wa-vv3h** (Rename project): Ship readiness depends on the final project name being settled. CI workflows, binary names, crate names, and documentation must all reflect the chosen name. Coordinate timing so that the rename lands before or as part of the v0.1.0 release to avoid publishing under a temporary name. README, quickstart, and `wa --version` output must use the canonical project name.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.9.1","title":"CI pipeline: fmt/clippy/test/bench (feature matrix for mcp/web/tui/sync/browser)","description":"# Task: CI pipeline

## Goal
Ensure core quality checks run automatically on changes and failures are diagnosable from logs/artifacts.

## Core checks (blocking)
- `cargo fmt --check`
- `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo test`

## Feature matrix smoke (blocking or warn-only; decide explicitly)
Build/test the crate under common feature combinations so optional integrations don’t silently rot.

Baseline matrix (PLAN Appendix A/F driven):
- default features
- `--features mcp`
- `--features web`
- `--features tui`
- `--features sync`
- `--features browser`

Vendored lane (PLAN Appendix H):
- When `--features vendored` exists, CI must at least:
  - `cargo check --all-targets --features vendored`
  - and run a minimal smoke/unit subset that exercises vendored wiring

This is intentionally separated from the ROI decision (`wa-nu4.4.1.6`): once vendored is enabled, CI must keep it from rotting.

## Docs correctness lane
- Add a docs-smoke step/job that keeps README quickstart commands executable (tracked by `wa-nu4.3.9.9`).

## Related CI jobs (separate tasks)
- E2E job for real WezTerm scenarios: `wa-nu4.3.9.6`
- Setup idempotency job (safe dotfile patching in temp home): `wa-nu4.3.9.8`
- Vendored maintenance lane: `wa-nu4.4.1.7`

## Artifacts and logging
- On failure, upload:
  - test logs (stdout/stderr)
  - E2E artifacts (runner logs + captured outputs)
  - benchmark outputs when applicable

## Testing
- CI “tests the tests”:
  - Add a minimal job that prints the exact rustc/cargo versions and the exact feature-matrix commands being executed.
  - Ensure CI captures and uploads logs/artifacts for failing jobs so failures are actionable.

## Acceptance Criteria
- CI failures are actionable (clear logs, no flaky tests).
- Optional features (especially vendored) do not silently break over time.

 {"id":"wa-nu4.3.9.2","title":"Docs: README/quickstart + architecture + CLI/robot/MCP reference","description":"# Task: Documentation for v0.1

## Goal
Make the project approachable for a new contributor and operable for users.

This bead is intentionally self-contained and mirrors PLAN Appendix A + F so contributors don’t need to dig through planning docs.

## Deliverables
### 1) README (operator-first)
- What wa is (WezTerm mux “terminal hypervisor” for agent fleets).
- Quickstart:
  - install wa
  - `wa setup`
  - `wa watch`
  - `wa status`
  - `wa query` / `wa events`
- Safety guarantees:
  - observe vs act split
  - no silent gaps
  - policy-gated sending + approvals

### 2) Architecture doc
- Ingest → Storage (SQLite+FTS5) → Pattern engine → Workflow engine → Robot/MCP.
- Deterministic state via OSC 133.
- Explicit GAP semantics.

### 3) CLI reference (PLAN Appendix A)
Human-oriented commands (rich output by default via `rich_rust`):
- `wa status` — panes + inferred agent state
- `wa watch` — run daemon (foreground/background)
- `wa stop` — stop watcher in workspace safely
- `wa events` — recent/unhandled events
- `wa query` — FTS search
- `wa send` — guarded send to a pane
- `wa reserve` — reserve a pane for exclusive use
- `wa reservations` — list active reservations
- `wa approve` — grant allow-once approvals
- `wa workflow` — run a workflow manually (guarded)
- `wa audit` — audit trail of actions (redacted)
- `wa history` — action timeline + undoability
- `wa undo` — undo supported actions (with confirmation)
- `wa rules` — list/test rule packs, show matching traces
- `wa accounts` — list/refresh accounts, show rotation picks
- `wa setup` — canonical WezTerm configuration (local/remote)
- `wa doctor` — environment checks (wezterm presence, DB health)
- `wa diag bundle` — sanitized diagnostic bundle export
- `wa export` — export slices/history/audit
- `wa web` — optional HTTP server (`fastapi_rust`) if enabled
- `wa tui` — optional TUI (`charmed_rust`) if enabled
- `wa sync` — optional sync (`asupersync`) if enabled

Robot mode commands (stable JSON envelope; token-efficient):
- `wa robot state [--domain <name>] [--agent <type>]`
- `wa robot get-text <pane_id> [--tail N] [--escapes]`
- `wa robot send <pane_id> "<text>" [--no-newline] [--wait-for "<pat>"] [--timeout-secs N]`
- `wa robot wait-for <pane_id> "<pat>" [--timeout-secs N]`
- `wa robot search "<fts query>" [--pane-id <id>] [--since <iso8601>] [--limit N]`
- `wa robot events [--unhandled] [--pane-id <id>] [--type <event>] [--limit N]`
- `wa robot workflow <name> <pane_id> [--force]`
- `wa robot reserve <pane_id> --ttl <secs> --reason <text>`
- `wa robot release <pane_id>`
- `wa robot reservations`
- `wa robot accounts [--service <openai|anthropic|google>]`
- `wa robot accounts refresh [--service <openai|anthropic|google>]`
- `wa robot rules list [--pack <name>]`
- `wa robot rules test "<text>" [--agent <type>]`
- `wa robot quick-start`

### 4) MCP reference (PLAN Appendix A)
Tools (names are short and obvious):
- `wa.state`
- `wa.get_text`
- `wa.send`
- `wa.wait_for`
- `wa.search`
- `wa.events`
- `wa.workflow_run`
- `wa.accounts`
- `wa.accounts_refresh`
- `wa.rules_list`
- `wa.rules_test`
- `wa.reserve`
- `wa.release`
- `wa.reservations`

Resources:
- `wa://panes`
- `wa://events`
- `wa://accounts`
- `wa://workflows`
- `wa://rules`
- `wa://reservations`

### 5) Library integration map (PLAN Appendix F)
| Library | Role in `wa` |
|---------|--------------|
| `cass` (`/dp/coding_agent_session_search`) | Correlation + session archaeology; used in status + workflows |
| `caut` (`/dp/coding_agent_usage_tracker`) | Usage truth + selection; used in accounts + `handle_usage_limits` |
| `rich_rust` | Human-first CLI output (tables/panels/highlight) |
| `charmed_rust` | Optional interactive TUI (pane picker, event feed, transcript viewer) |
| `fastmcp_rust` | MCP tool surface for agent control (mirrors robot mode) |
| `fastapi_rust` | Optional HTTP server for dashboards/webhooks (read-only first) |
| `asupersync` | Remote bootstrap/sync layer (configs, binaries, DB snapshots) |
| `playwright` | Automate device auth flows with persistent profiles |
| `ast-grep` | Structure-aware codebase scans in “unstick agent” workflows |

## Testing / doc correctness
Docs must not drift.

- The quickstart commands shown in README should be validated regularly:
  - either by referencing existing E2E scripts, or
  - by adding a small “docs smoke” CI step that runs the exact commands from the docs in a controlled environment (tracked by `wa-nu4.3.9.9`).

- Robot mode docs should match the schema contract:
  - reference the versioned JSON schemas and keep field names consistent.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A new engineer can get to a running watcher and see events with no oral tradition.

+#!/usr/bin/env bash
+# E2E: validate deterministic leak-oracle regressions for ft-xbnl0.4.4.
+set -euo pipefail
+
+ROOT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")/../.." && pwd)"
+BEAD_ID="ft-xbnl0.4.4"
+SCENARIO_ID="leak_oracle_regressions"
+RUN_ID="$(date -u +"%Y%m%dT%H%M%SZ")"
+CORRELATION_ID="${BEAD_ID}-${RUN_ID}"
+HARNESS_NAME="ft_xbnl0_4_4_leak_oracle_regressions"
+ARTIFACT_DIR="${ROOT_DIR}/tests/e2e/artifacts/goal-line/${BEAD_ID}/${SCENARIO_ID}/${RUN_ID}"
+mkdir -p "${ARTIFACT_DIR}"
+
+COMMANDS_FILE="${ARTIFACT_DIR}/commands.txt"
+ENV_FILE="${ARTIFACT_DIR}/env.txt"
+STRUCTURED_LOG="${ARTIFACT_DIR}/structured.log"
+STDOUT_FILE="${ARTIFACT_DIR}/stdout.txt"
+STDERR_FILE="${ARTIFACT_DIR}/stderr.txt"
+SUMMARY_FILE="${ARTIFACT_DIR}/summary.json"
+
+exec > >(tee -a "${STDOUT_FILE}")
+exec 2> >(tee -a "${STDERR_FILE}" >&2)
+
+source "${ROOT_DIR}/tests/e2e/lib_rch_guards.sh"
+rch_init "${ARTIFACT_DIR}" "${RUN_ID}" "${HARNESS_NAME}" "${ROOT_DIR}"
+RCH_SKIP_SMOKE_PREFLIGHT=1
+
+PASS=0
+FAIL=0
+TOTAL=0
+REMOTE_TARGET_DIR="/tmp/ft-$(whoami)-target"
+
+record_command() {
+    printf '%s\n' "$*" >> "${COMMANDS_FILE}"
+}
+
+write_env() {
+    {
+        printf 'timestamp=%s\n' "$(date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")"
+        printf 'bead_id=%s\n' "${BEAD_ID}"
+        printf 'scenario_id=%s\n' "${SCENARIO_ID}"
+        printf 'correlation_id=%s\n' "${CORRELATION_ID}"
+        printf 'artifact_dir=%s\n' "${ARTIFACT_DIR}"
+        printf 'platform=%s\n' "$(uname -srm)"
+        printf 'cwd=%s\n' "${ROOT_DIR}"
+        printf 'remote_cargo_target_dir=%s\n' "${REMOTE_TARGET_DIR}"
+        printf 'rch_skip_smoke_preflight=%s\n' "${RCH_SKIP_SMOKE_PREFLIGHT}"
+        printf 'rch_probe_log=%s\n' "$(rch_probe_log_path)"
+        printf 'rch_smoke_log=%s\n' "$(rch_smoke_log_path)"
+    } > "${ENV_FILE}"
+}
+
+emit_log() {
+    local step="$1"
+    local status="$2"
+    local duration_ms="$3"
+    local message="$4"
+    jq -cn \
+        --arg timestamp "$(date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")" \
+        --arg bead_id "${BEAD_ID}" \
+        --arg scenario_id "${SCENARIO_ID}" \
+        --arg surface "runtime-leak-oracle" \
+        --arg step "${step}" \
+        --arg status "${status}" \
+        --arg correlation_id "${CORRELATION_ID}" \
+        --arg backend "rch" \
+        --arg platform "$(uname -srm)" \
+        --arg artifact_dir "${ARTIFACT_DIR}" \
+        --arg redaction "none" \
+        --arg message "${message}" \
+        --argjson duration_ms "${duration_ms}" \
+        '{
+          timestamp: $timestamp,
+          bead_id: $bead_id,
+          scenario_id: $scenario_id,
+          surface: $surface,
+          step: $step,
+          status: $status,
+          duration_ms: $duration_ms,
+          correlation_id: $correlation_id,
+          backend: $backend,
+          platform: $platform,
+          artifact_dir: $artifact_dir,
+          redaction: $redaction,
+          message: $message
+        }' >> "${STRUCTURED_LOG}"
+}
+
+record_result() {
+    local step="$1"
+    local ok="$2"
+    local duration_ms="$3"
+    local message="$4"
+    TOTAL=$((TOTAL + 1))
+    if [[ "${ok}" == "true" ]]; then
+        PASS=$((PASS + 1))
+        emit_log "${step}" "passed" "${duration_ms}" "${message}"
+        printf 'PASS %s\n' "${step}"
+    else
+        FAIL=$((FAIL + 1))
+        emit_log "${step}" "failed" "${duration_ms}" "${message}"
+        printf 'FAIL %s\n' "${step}" >&2
+    fi
+}
+
+run_checked() {
+    local step="$1"
+    local log_file="$2"
+    shift 2
+    local start_ns end_ns duration_ms
+    start_ns="$(date +%s%N)"
+    record_command "$*"
+    if "$@" > "${log_file}" 2>&1; then
+        end_ns="$(date +%s%N)"
+        duration_ms="$(((end_ns - start_ns) / 1000000))"
+        record_result "${step}" "true" "${duration_ms}" "${log_file}"
+        return 0
+    fi
+    end_ns="$(date +%s%N)"
+    duration_ms="$(((end_ns - start_ns) / 1000000))"
+    record_result "${step}" "false" "${duration_ms}" "${log_file}"
+    return 1
+}
+
+run_rch_step() {
+    local step="$1"
+    local log_file="$2"
+    shift 2
+    local start_ns end_ns duration_ms
+    start_ns="$(date +%s%N)"
+    record_command "rch exec -- $*"
+    if run_rch_cargo_logged "${log_file}" "$@"; then
+        end_ns="$(date +%s%N)"
+        duration_ms="$(((end_ns - start_ns) / 1000000))"
+        record_result "${step}" "true" "${duration_ms}" "${log_file}"
+        return 0
+    fi
+    end_ns="$(date +%s%N)"
+    duration_ms="$(((end_ns - start_ns) / 1000000))"
+    record_result "${step}" "false" "${duration_ms}" "${log_file}"
+    return 1
+}
+
+echo "=== ${BEAD_ID} deterministic leak-oracle regressions ==="
+write_env
+command -v jq >/dev/null 2>&1
+command -v rg >/dev/null 2>&1
+command -v rch >/dev/null 2>&1
+command -v rustfmt >/dev/null 2>&1
+record_command "ensure_rch_ready (RCH_SKIP_SMOKE_PREFLIGHT=${RCH_SKIP_SMOKE_PREFLIGHT})"
+ensure_rch_ready
+rch_write_meta_json "$(rch_probe_log_path)"
+rch_write_meta_json "$(rch_smoke_log_path)"
+
+AUDIT_LOG="${ARTIFACT_DIR}/source_audit.log"
+run_checked \
+    "source_audit" \
+    "${AUDIT_LOG}" \
+    bash -lc "
+        set -euo pipefail
+        rg -n 'ft_xbnl0_4_4_leak_inventory_returns_to_baseline_after_pane_teardown|ft_xbnl0_4_4_runtime_state_compaction_stays_bounded_across_churn_cycles' \
+            '${ROOT_DIR}/crates/frankenterm-core/src/runtime.rs'
+        rg -n 'ft_xbnl0_4_4_tick_keeps_watermarks_bounded_across_churn_cycles' \
+            '${ROOT_DIR}/crates/frankenterm-core/src/search/indexing_pipeline.rs'
+    "
+
+FMT_LOG="${ARTIFACT_DIR}/rustfmt_check.log"
+run_checked \
+    "rustfmt_check" \
+    "${FMT_LOG}" \
+    rustfmt --edition 2024 --check \
+        "${ROOT_DIR}/crates/frankenterm-core/src/runtime.rs" \
+        "${ROOT_DIR}/crates/frankenterm-core/src/search/indexing_pipeline.rs"
+
+LIB_TEST_LOG="${ARTIFACT_DIR}/frankenterm_core_lib_tests.log"
+run_rch_step \
+    "frankenterm_core_lib_tests" \
+    "${LIB_TEST_LOG}" \
+    env CARGO_TARGET_DIR="${REMOTE_TARGET_DIR}" \
+        cargo test -p frankenterm-core --lib ft_xbnl0_4_4_ -- --nocapture
+rch_write_meta_json "${LIB_TEST_LOG}"
+
+CHECK_LOG="${ARTIFACT_DIR}/frankenterm_core_lib_check.log"
+run_rch_step \
+    "frankenterm_core_lib_check" \
+    "${CHECK_LOG}" \
+    env CARGO_TARGET_DIR="${REMOTE_TARGET_DIR}" \
+        cargo check -p frankenterm-core --lib
+rch_write_meta_json "${CHECK_LOG}"
+
+jq -cn \
+    --arg bead_id "${BEAD_ID}" \
+    --arg scenario_id "${SCENARIO_ID}" \
+    --arg correlation_id "${CORRELATION_ID}" \
+    --arg artifact_dir "${ARTIFACT_DIR}" \
+    --arg commands_file "${COMMANDS_FILE}" \
+    --arg env_file "${ENV_FILE}" \
+    --arg structured_log "${STRUCTURED_LOG}" \
+    --arg stdout_file "${STDOUT_FILE}" \
+    --arg stderr_file "${STDERR_FILE}" \
+    --arg rch_probe_log "$(rch_probe_log_path)" \
+    --arg rch_probe_meta "$(rch_log_meta_path "$(rch_probe_log_path)")" \
+    --arg rch_smoke_log "$(rch_smoke_log_path)" \
+    --arg rch_smoke_meta "$(rch_log_meta_path "$(rch_smoke_log_path)")" \
+    --arg audit_log "${AUDIT_LOG}" \
+    --arg fmt_log "${FMT_LOG}" \
+    --arg lib_test_log "${LIB_TEST_LOG}" \
+    --arg lib_test_meta "$(rch_log_meta_path "${LIB_TEST_LOG}")" \
+    --arg check_log "${CHECK_LOG}" \
+    --arg check_meta "$(rch_log_meta_path "${CHECK_LOG}")" \
+    --argjson pass_count "${PASS}" \
+    --argjson fail_count "${FAIL}" \
+    --argjson total_count "${TOTAL}" \
+    '{
+      bead_id: $bead_id,
+      scenario_id: $scenario_id,
+      status: (if $fail_count == 0 then "passed" else "failed" end),
+      correlation_id: $correlation_id,
+      artifact_dir: $artifact_dir,
+      pass_count: $pass_count,
+      fail_count: $fail_count,
+      total_count: $total_count,
+      artifacts: {
+        commands: $commands_file,
+        env: $env_file,
+        structured_log: $structured_log,
+        stdout: $stdout_file,
+        stderr: $stderr_file,
+        rch_probe: $rch_probe_log,
+        rch_probe_meta: $rch_probe_meta,
+        rch_smoke: $rch_smoke_log,
+        rch_smoke_meta: $rch_smoke_meta,
+        source_audit: $audit_log,
+        rustfmt_check: $fmt_log,
+        frankenterm_core_lib_tests: $lib_test_log,
+        frankenterm_core_lib_tests_meta: $lib_test_meta,
+        frankenterm_core_lib_check: $check_log,
+        frankenterm_core_lib_check_meta: $check_meta
+      }
+    }' > "${SUMMARY_FILE}"
+
+if [[ "${FAIL}" -ne 0 ]]; then
+    echo "ft-xbnl0.4.4 leak-oracle regression verification FAILED. Summary: ${SUMMARY_FILE}" >&2
+    exit 1
+fi
+
+echo "ft-xbnl0.4.4 leak-oracle regression verification passed. Summary: ${SUMMARY_FILE}"
